title: Nettle infusion for cycle support
subtitle: A mineral-rich tonic infusion for the second half of the cycle
excerpt: A daily hot infusion of dried nettle leaf, taken in the week before menstruation as a nutritive tonic for the cycle. Nettle is rich in iron, calcium, and magnesium, the minerals most depleted by menstruation, and has a long tradition as a cycle-support herb in western herbalism.
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve, A Modern Herbal (1931), nettle entry, nutritive tonic properties, haemostatic use, iron and mineral content. Traditional use of nettle as a blood and cycle tonic in European domestic medicine. EMA HMPC assessment of Urtica dioica folium (2011), confirms high mineral content including iron, magnesium, and calcium.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Dried nettle leaf keeps 12 months in a sealed tin. Brew each cup fresh.

glossaryTerms.definition: A herb that provides meaningful amounts of vitamins and minerals alongside its medicinal action, taken as a food-herb rather than purely as a medicine.

content.text: A daily cup of nettle leaf tea in the week before menstruation. Nettle is the herbal tradition's foremost

, its leaves are rich in bioavailable iron, calcium, magnesium, and silica, the minerals most depleted by menstruation. Taking nettle daily in the luteal phase (the second half of the cycle, from ovulation to the period) replenishes mineral stores before they are lost and may reduce the fatigue, irritability, and low energy that some women experience premenstrually. Maud Grieve notes nettle's nutritive and blood-building properties at length. This is a tonic preparation, taken for its nutritional and general toning value rather than as an acute treatment.

attrs.title: Important safety notes
attrs.body:
Not medical advice. Consult a qualified herbalist or doctor for ongoing or serious symptoms.

Nettle leaf at normal infusion strength is well tolerated and safe for regular use. The main interaction noted in the literature is with warfarin, nettle contains vitamin K, and very high regular intake (multiple litres per day) could theoretically affect INR. One to two cups per day is unlikely to be significant, but inform your prescriber if on warfarin.

Pregnancy: nettle leaf infusion is widely used in pregnancy and is considered safe at normal tea strength, it is a traditional preparation for nourishing the blood during pregnancy. As with all herbs, regular therapeutic use in the first trimester is best discussed with a midwife.

See your doctor for irregular or absent periods, unusually heavy periods, severe premenstrual symptoms significantly affecting daily life, or any menstrual irregularity that has changed in pattern. These need proper investigation.

items.prepNote: about 1 heaped teaspoon of dried leaf
optional, nettle tea is mild and slightly earthy

content.text: Place {{dried-nettle-leaf}} of dried nettle leaf in the teapot or infuser. Pour {{water}} of just-off-boil water over and lid immediately. Steep for 10 minutes. Strain and drink. The taste is mild, green, and slightly grassy, more pleasant than many herbal teas, and well suited to daily regular drinking.

One to two cups daily throughout the second half of the cycle (from ovulation until the period begins) and continuing through the first few days of the period when mineral loss is highest. Nettle is safe for daily long-term use, there are no significant safety concerns at one to two cups per day. The tonic effect is cumulative: the benefits are most apparent after two to three cycles of consistent use rather than immediately.
When NOT to use this remedy

See your doctor for significant changes to your menstrual cycle, heavier bleeding, missed periods, worsening premenstrual symptoms, before assuming they are addressable by nutritional support alone. Inform your prescriber if on warfarin and beginning a regular nettle infusion. The nettle infusion is a nutritive tonic, not a treatment for menstrual disorders.
